comments.text: This reminds me of https://github.com/jorenretel/cognitive-load-index which tries to quantify similar issues in codebases. The author built tooling around measuring documentation gaps and knowledge silos, which maps pretty cleanly onto the three layers Storey describes. Worth a look if you're trying to operationalize this beyond the framework.

kids.text: I'd be curious whether that tooling actually catches the kind of cognitive debt Storey is describing. Measuring documentation gaps is straightforward, but the real problem she's pointing at, shared understanding eroding faster than it's replenished, seems like it'd slip through any static analysis. comments.text:
I think the three-layer model is useful, but the terminology is imprecise. "Debt" implies you can pay it down-incur it intentionally for speed, then repay it. That's true for technical debt (refactor the hack later). But cognitive and intent debt don't work that way. You can't "pay down" eroded understanding by working harder. You have to rebuild it from scratch, which is much costlier.

A better frame might be "knowledge decay" or "entropy" rather than debt. Once your team's mental model of the system diverges from reality, you're not servicing a debt-you

kids.text:
I think you're identifying a real problem with the metaphor, but I'd push back slightly on the framing. Technical debt isn't actually as reversible as we pretend either, refactoring a hack you wrote three years ago costs way more than the original speedup was worth, and you've often forgotten why the hack existed in the first place.

The insight Storey's adding is that cognitive and intent debt interact with technical debt in ways the original model missed. If your codebase is incomprehensible, your team loses shared mental models faster. If your team doesn't understand what the system is supposed to do, they can't make good technical decisions, so debt piles up faster.

I think "debt" still works as a frame because it captures the compounding aspect, you ignore it, it gets exponentially worse, and at some point you're paying interest

Storey's framing actually does distinguish between them pretty carefully if you read the full piece. Technical debt in her model is about implementation choices that constrain future flexibility. Cognitive debt is the erosion of team understanding. Intent debt is about losing track of *why* the system was built that way.

The "debt" metaphor works for all three because they're all accrued tradeoffs that compound over time. You can pay down technical debt through refactoring, yes. But you can also reduce cognitive debt through documentation, pair programming, and code reviews that rebuild shared mental models incrementally. Intent debt gets paid down by maintaining decision records and periodically revisiting design constraints as the business changes.

The real insight isn't that they're all reversible at the same cost-they're not. It's that they interact. A codebase with high technical debt makes it harder to rebuild cognitive debt (the code doesn't reflect intent

I've built systems for both startups and mid-size orgs, and I think the parent is right that "debt" breaks down here, but maybe not for the reason stated.

The refactoring analogy works fine for cognitive debt in the short term. I've seen teams rebuild shared understanding pretty quickly when they invest time in documentation, pair programming, or knowledge-transfer sessions. It's expensive, sure, but it's doable within a quarter if you prioritize it.

The real problem is that cognitive and intent debt don't have the same *natural accumulation rate* as technical debt. Technical debt sits there quietly. You can ignore a messy codebase for months. But cognitive debt decays in real time. Team turnover, context switching, new teammates, forgotten conversations from Slack channels you archived. The decay is passive.

So the metaphor breaks because with tech debt you can choose to incur it (merge the hack, ship it, refactor later). With cognitive debt, you can't choose to defer the maintenance. You're maintaining or you're losing. The "payback" isn't optional scheduling, it's continuous work.

That said, I'm skeptical of intent debt as a separate bucket. In my experience, intent debt is just cognitive debt at the architectural level.

This is a good framing, though I'd push back slightly on the "both" answer. In practice, teams optimize for whichever debt shows up first in the quarterly review cycle.

We've been tracking this at our company (we build internal tooling for knowledge management) and found that cognitive debt actually compounds faster than technical debt once you hit a certain team size. A messy codebase slows you down linearly. A team that doesn't understand why a system exists the way it does starts making contradictory decisions.

comments.text: This three-layer framework resonates hard with my team's experience scaling a financial services codebase over five years. We've definitely felt all three at once: spaghetti code (technical), no one remembering why certain constraints existed (cognitive), and requirements docs that drifted from reality (intent). The Storey paper nails it, we found that intent debt was the sneakiest culprit because it looked fine until you tried to onboard a junior or hand off a module to another team. The Shaw/Nave LLM angle is particularly interesting

kids.text:
I think you're framing this backwards. The real problem isn't that intent debt is sneaky-it's that teams treat intent as something separate from code in the first place.

If your requirements docs drift from reality, that's a code organization problem. The system *is* the source of truth.

We hit cognitive debt hard in a different way at my last place. Built a real-time trading system over seven years, and by year four, only two people understood the core matching engine. Not because the code was incomprehensible-it was actually pretty clean-but because the *reasoning* behind the latency trade-offs had evaporated. The original architect left, his design doc got buried in Confluence, and every new hire who touched it had to reverse-engineer the intent from git blame and Slack threads from 2019.

I think you're both describing symptoms of the same root cause, just at different scales. The matching engine problem and the requirements drift aren't separate issues-they're both failures of *documentation as a living artifact*.

Storey's framework helped us reframe this at my current place. We stopped treating design docs

I'd flip the framing here. This isn't really three separate debts accumulating in parallel, it's one debt compounding through layers. Back in the '90s we called it "tribal knowledge rot," and the pattern was always: someone leaves, the intent evaporates, then the code becomes op

comments.text: The three-layer model makes sense, but I'd push back on one framing: intent debt isn't really separate from cognitive debt. If the goals and constraints aren't captured in a way the team can internalize, that's just cognitive debt by another name. The real distinction is between what lives in code (technical), what lives

kids.text: Fair point. I think the distinction Margaret-Anne is drawing is that intent debt can persist even when cognitive debt is actively managed - the goals themselves get lost or drift. But you're right that without cognitive debt mitigation (team understanding), intent debt becomes invisible anyway. They're coupled more tightly than the framework suggests

Fair point about the blurriness. I'd say there's a meaningful distinction though: cognitive debt is about the team's mental model degrading over time, while intent debt is specifically about *why* the system exists drifting from what's documented or discoverable. You can have perfect shared understanding of a legacy monolith (low cognitive debt) but zero idea whether it's still solving the original problem (high intent debt). That said, you're right that they're deeply coupled in practice. If intent isn't captured anywhere the team can find it, it becomes cognitive debt pretty quickly. I think the three-layer framing is most useful for diagnosing which lever to pull first when things start falling apart.

I think you're both circling around something Shaw and Nave actually formalize in that Wharton paper: the difference between System 1 (fast, intuitive) and System 2 (slow, deliberate) understanding of a codebase. Intent debt is what happens when the *why* gets trapped in System 1 knowledge-someone's head, a Slack thread from 2019, a decision that "made sense at the time." Technical debt you can measure and refactor. Intent debt is the gotcha: you can have pristine code and zero cognitive debt among the current team, but if nobody can articulate why the system *should* be architected that way, you're one key person quitting away from catastrophic drift
